reasoning:
The question asks if there will be more than 10 times the baseline fatalities (which equates to 11 or more fatalities) in any 30-day period prior to three resolution dates in Mongolia. The base rate for such 10x-spikes in ACLED data is extremely low (0.002). Mongolia is historically very peaceful; its worst modern political violence—the 2008 post-election riots—resulted in only 5 deaths. While there is an ongoing political and constitutional crisis as of October 2025 (with the previous PM resigning in June after 21 days of protests and the new PM ousted by parliament in October), the massive protests have consistently been non-violent. Reaching 11+ fatalities would require an unprecedented outbreak of political violence at more than double the scale of the 2008 incident. 

Furthermore, the first resolution date (Nov 2) leaves only 7 days for such violence to occur following the knowledge cutoff date, making it highly improbable. The later dates (Nov 25 and Jan 24) fall progressively deeper into Mongolia's notoriously harsh winter; Ulaanbaatar is the coldest capital in the world, with January temperatures routinely dropping below -25°C, making sustained, large-scale outdoor civil unrest physically challenging. Following the requirement to have uncertainty strictly increase with exactly the same baseline evidence out to the longest horizon, I have kept the probabilities close to the minimum bound.
